w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: Assessing the neighborhood impacts of Verticillium wilt of
PhD Candidate (ORCID 0009-0006-8380-1314), Department of Forest Resources and Environmental Conservation, Virginia Tech, Blacksburg, Virginia, USA;
Professor, School of Plant and Environmental Sciences, Virginia Tech, Blacksburg, Virginia, USA;
Professor, Department of Forest Resources and Environmental Conservation, Virginia Tech, Blacksburg, Virginia, USA;
Professor, Department of Entomology, Virginia Tech, Blacksburg, Virginia, USA;
Assistant Professor, Department of Forest Resources and Environmental Conservation, Virginia Tech, Blacksburg, Virginia, USA
Biological control of the invasive tree

Inderb. et al., specifically the virulent strain VnAa140, can rapidly kill host trees, but whether the resulting disease alters soil in ways that affect surrounding vegetation is unknown. This study examined whether proximity to diseased
is associated with changes in the soil environment detectable through plant performance. At a single Virginia field site, three plots of clustered

stems were each assigned one inoculation treatment: an attenuated strain of VnAa140, a virulent strain, or a fungus-free control. Soil was sampled at five spatially distributed points per plot before inoculation and again two and four months after inoculation. Four bioassay species were grown ex situ in the field-collected soil:
(L.) R.M. King & H. Rob. (white snakeroot), and
L. (common yarrow). For each soil sample, a disease-weighted

neighborhood index, termed disease influence, was computed, summing each tree’s basal area and disease load discounted by distance. The within-plot gradient of this index was tested against seedling length, root-to-shoot ratio, and germination, separately for each species. No response was significantly associated with disease influence at α = 0.05. The only suggestive trends were modest declines in root-to-shoot ratio for black-eyed Susan (p = 0.059) and yarrow (p = 0.065); for every length and germination model a plot-only null was preferred. Within this design, no single-season, soil-mediated effect of Verticillium wilt in
was detected, underscoring the nominal non-target effects of
and providing evidence in support of its release as an augmentative biological control.
bioassay, biological control, germination, invasive species, mycoherbicide, root-to-shoot ratio, tree-of-heaven, wilt disease

Land managers increasingly view Verticillium wilt as a tool for suppressing tree-of-heaven, one of North America’s most widespread invasive trees. A practical worry with any pathogen-based control is what happens belowground once the target trees sicken and die, whether the dying stand leaves a legacy that helps desirable natives reestablish or instead one that favors aggressive competitors. The results of this study speak to the indirect effects on soil that may occur following inoculation of tree-of-heaven with

strain VnAa140. Across seeded test species spanning restoration natives and an aggressive weedy grass, early germination and seedling growth tracked no measurable gradient in local disease pressure. Managers should therefore not expect the soil immediately around diseased trees to become either a head start for native seedlings or weed flushes within the first year of treatment.
Two lessons follow. First, restoration that depends on a favorable post-treatment soil should not be timed to the first season: soil legacies build and fade over years, not weeks, so any shift from

decline is likely to emerge, if at all, only after repeated seasons, and revegetation plans should budget for multi-year monitoring and follow-up seeding rather than a single post-inoculation window. Second, because no soil-mediated release of the competitors tested was detected, the familiar drivers of competitor surges after canopy loss (light, space, and the existing seed bank) remain the priorities to manage directly.

), a deciduous tree of the Simaroubaceae native to China, is among the most widely distributed invasive trees in temperate regions worldwide (Kowarik and Säumel 2007). Introduced to North America near Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, in 1784 (Hu 1979, Kowarik and Säumel 2007), it now occurs across much of the continental United States in forests, riparian corridors, agricultural margins, and urban and otherwise disturbed sites. Its invasive success is commonly attributed to rapid juvenile growth, prolific wind- and water-dispersed seed production, vigorous clonal spread from root sprouts, broad environmental tolerance, and an aggressive resprouting response to stress (Kowarik and Säumel 2007).
Beyond direct competition for light and space,

exerts pronounced effects on the soil it occupies. The species is widely regarded as allelopathic: its root bark, foliage, and litter contain the quassinoid ailanthone and related secondary compounds that inhibit germination and seedling growth of numerous co-occurring species (Heisey 1996). Ailanthone is, however, rapidly degraded in non-sterile soils, and the strength of allelopathic interference under field conditions remains debated, with in situ effects that appear more transient relative to those measured in laboratory bioassays (Heisey and Heisey 2003, Gómez-Aparicio and Canham 2008).

More broadly, invasive plants can reshape belowground systems by altering litter inputs, root exudation, nutrient pools, and the composition and activity of soil microbial communities; such changes frequently generate feedbacks that reinforce invader dominance and can persist as soil legacies after removal of the invader (Ehrenfeld 2003, Callaway and Ridenour 2004, Liao et al. 2008, Weidenhamer and Callaway 2010).
is consistent with this pattern: invaded soils can differ from adjacent uninvaded soils in pH, nitrogen availability, and other nutrient

pools, and the species has been shown to shift the structure of soil bacterial communities and to alter microbial activity (Medina-Villar et al. 2016). Because soil microorganisms mediate decomposition, nutrient mineralization, and plant–soil feedbacks,
–associated changes in soil biota and chemistry have consequences for the trajectory of invaded sites and for their potential to recover (Ehrenfeld 2003).

is costly and frequently incomplete: cutting alone stimulates prolific resprouting, and durable control typically requires repeated or systemic herbicide application (Kowarik and Säumel 2007). Against this backdrop, the soilborne vascular wilt fungus
Inderb. et al. has emerged as a promising augmentative biological control agent. Virulent strains were first recovered from naturally occurring diseased
epicenters in Pennsylvania (Schall and Davis 2009a) and have since been documented killing

in Ohio (Rebbeck et al. 2013), Virginia (Snyder et al. 2014), and in Europe (Maschek and Halmschlager 2018). The disease is acute: inoculated trees typically progress from chlorosis to wilt, defoliation with epicormic sprouting, and death, often within a single growing season (Schall and Davis 2009a, Kasson et al. 2014, Brooks et al. 2020, Shively et al. 2025). Strict inoculation protocols, host-range testing, and risk assessments indicate that
strain VnAa140 (NRRL 66861) is highly virulent to

while posing limited pathogenic risk to most co-occurring species (Schall and Davis 2009b, Kasson et al. 2014, 2015, O’Neal and Davis 2015). The strain, VnAa140, used in the present study is the most well-characterized virulent isolate currently under consideration for general release (Kasson et al. 2019, Miles et al. 2024).
as a biological control agent has focused largely on its virulence to
host specificity, and transmission, whereas its consequences for the soil
environment of treated stands remain largely unexamined. This gap is notable for two reasons. First,

is naturally a soilborne fungus, which, despite a precise stem injection used for biological control, may enter soil when expelled from the host’s roots (Fradin and Thomma 2006). Consequently, its use means introducing a known pathogen to the soil microbial community of treated stands. Second, because
modifies soil chemistry and microbial communities, disease-induced decline of the tree could alter those same soil properties as
inputs cease and large volumes of dying root and shoot tissue enter the system. Whether wilt-induced
mortality changes the soil ecology of invaded sites, and over what timescale, has not been directly tested. Thus, the objective of this study was to determine whether biological control of

alters the soil ecology of invaded stands over a single growing season with the expectation that, as inoculated trees developed disease and declined, soil collected from points under greater local disease influence would produce different germination and seedling-growth responses relative to soil collected from points under lower disease influence within the same plot.

All inoculations and soil sampling were conducted at the Chickahominy Wildlife Management Area near Williamsburg, Virginia, USA, which is managed for recreation by the Virginia Department of Wildlife Resources. All study plots were in a tree line bordering an approximately 1 ha mowed area managed for bird hunting (37.317396, -76.886177). The site has a mean annual temperature of 15.7°C and 1262.3 mm mean annual precipitation (PRISM Climate Group 2024) and lies within USDA hardiness zone 8a (USDA-ARS 2023). Herbaceous growth in the
plots was dominated by Japanese stiltgrass (

was the dominant canopy tree in each plot. Soils at all plots are characterized as Kempsville loamy sand (Soil Survey Staff 2024). Soil samples from each plot were submitted to the Virginia Tech Soil Testing Laboratory for characterization.
Inoculum was prepared from two isolates of
strain VnAa140, which was originally isolated from symptomatic

in Pennsylvania and permitted for use in Virginia by the USDA Animal and Plant Health Inspection Service (APHIS; permit P526P-22-05428). The first isolate, VnAa140-19R4, was used for field inoculations throughout Virginia in 2022 and was found to be highly attenuated though still pathogenic; it was passed through a host four times in the laboratory (hence the “R4” designation), meaning it was used to inoculate

seedlings, re-isolated, and used to inoculate another seedling. Over these serial host passages, virulence appeared to be restored. The second isolate, VnAa140-23R2, consistently induced wilt symptoms and mortality in seedlings and was so designated after two host passages. Inoculum for the control treatment consisted of sterile 0.1% peptone solution only. For readability, the treatments are hereafter referred to as attenuated (VnAa140-19R4), virulent (VnAa140-23R2), and control (0.1% peptone).

To prepare inoculum, pure colonies were grown for 14 d on prune extract agar (Talboys 1960) amended with streptomycin sulfate and neomycin sulfate. Colonies were flushed with a sterile solution of 0.1% peptone in distilled water and scraped loose with a flame-sterilized spatula. The resulting mixture was poured through an autoclaved polyester-blend milk filter with 0.05 mm pores (KenAG, Ashland, OH, USA) to remove hyphae and separate conidia. A
hemocytometer was used to dilute the spore suspension to a concentration of 1 × 10

. A droplet of inoculum was further diluted, streaked on agar, and incubated at room temperature for 24 h; inoculum viability was assessed under magnification by counting germinated conidia, with a minimum acceptance standard of 75%.
Inoculations were performed on May 6, 2024. Three plots were established, each consisting of at least 20 clustered

stems ≥ 8 cm diameter at breast height (DBH) and separated from one another by a minimum of 20 m to mitigate potential spread of the pathogen between treatments by natural means (e.g., root grafts). Each plot was randomly assigned to one of the three treatments (attenuated, virulent, or control). All stems were inventoried, and a subset of 10 stems per plot was selected for inoculation so that potential spread to uninoculated stems could be identified and monitored. Inoculations followed Maschek and Halmschlager (2016), using a disposable 1.5 mL plastic dropper, a rubber mallet, and a straight gouge (Pfeil Swiss Made Tools; 05D04 #5 Sweep Gouge 8 mm or 05E07 #7 Sweep Gouge 25 mm). Stems > 18 cm DBH received 3 mL of inoculum spread evenly among three wounds made with the #7 gouge, and stems ≤ 18 cm DBH received 1 mL of inoculum spread evenly between two wounds made with the #5 gouge (Figure 1). Wounds were made equidistant around each stem approximately 0.5 m above the ground.
Disease progression on the inoculated and uninoculated flagged

in each plot was monitored with a 1–6 disease severity scale (Brooks et al. 2020): 1 = asymptomatic, 2 = chlorotic, 3 = wilting, 4 = multiple symptoms (chlorosis, wilt, and < 50% defoliation), 5 = ≥ 50%
defoliation, and 6 = dead with no living foliage. Each
was visually assessed prior to inoculation
and again at four approximately monthly intervals after inoculation, the last coinciding with the September soil collection.

At each plot, soil was sampled from five stations: one centered on the plot and one located 10 m from plot center in each of the four cardinal directions. At each station, soil samples were collected at equidistant points around a 0.5 m circle: five samples per circle in the virulent and control plots and three samples per circle in the attenuated plot (Figure 2). At each sampling point, leaf litter was swept aside and approximately 1 L of soil was collected from the top 10 cm with an ethanol-sterilized Hori Hori knife. Samples were placed on dry ice in the field and stored at −80°C. Soil was sampled concurrently with inoculation on May 6, 2024, and again on July 1 and September 6, 2024.
Four species were used in the germination bioassay:
L. (black-eyed Susan; Ernst Conservation Seeds, Meadville, PA, USA),
(L.) Pers. (Johnsongrass; Azlin Seed Service, Leland, MS, USA),
(L.) R.M. King & H. Rob. (white snakeroot; Prairie Moon Nursery, Winona, MN, USA), and

L. (common yarrow; Roundstone Native Seed LLC, Upton, KY, USA). To target at least five viable seeds per assay, an initial germination test was conducted for each seed source: 100 seeds were placed on a double layer of moistened filter paper in clear plastic containers sealed with parafilm, with two replicates per species. After 5 d, the number of germinated seeds was counted, and the mean of the two replicates was used to determine the number of seeds to sow in each bioassay, rounded up to the nearest 10 for ease of counting (10, 100, 40, and 40 seeds for

Individual soil samples were thawed, homogenized by hand, and pooled by sampling station and collection date (May, July, or September), yielding five pooled samples per plot per date, or 45 pooled samples in total. Each pooled sample was added to a seed tray with four separate cells (Figure 3).

Seeds of each species were sown into separate cells in the numbers determined by the seed-stock germination test. All trays were initially watered to saturation with tap water through a mist nozzle and again three and five days later. Trays were arranged randomly on a table and re-shuffled three and five days after sowing to control for potential temperature and light variation across the table. Trays were kept outdoors under full shade.

After seven days, each emerged seedling was teased from the soil with fine forceps and rinsed in a sifting screen; the remaining soil was also rinsed through screens to recover germinated seeds that had not emerged. A seed was scored as germinated if there was any break in the seed coat through which a cotyledon or root was visible to the naked eye. Seedlings were straightened, and root and shoot lengths were measured separately to the nearest millimeter. In the rare event that an opened seed coat remained attached and obscured the plant tissue, a minimum length of 1 mm was recorded.
To relate each soil sample to the local intensity of
wilt, a continuous, disease-weighted neighbourhood index was derived for every soil-sampling point, beginning from the additive

neighborhood index (ANI) of Gómez-Aparicio and Canham (2008), in which the cumulative influence of a stand at a focal location is the sum, over neighbouring trees, of each tree’s size multiplied by an exponential function of its distance.

For each inoculated and flagged tree, the area under the disease-progress curve (AUDPC) was computed from its severity scores on the 1–6 scale after subtracting the tree’s pre-inoculation (asymptomatic) score from every reading and setting any negative difference to zero, so that a tree that never developed symptoms contributed nothing to disease influence. With
the severity score of tree i at reading k (k = 0 at inoculation and k = 1–4 at the four successive monthly readings) and
its pre-inoculation score, the baseline-adjusted severity
, 0) was accumulated over the readings by the trapezoidal rule:

where Δt = 1 month is the interval between successive readings, so that the baseline-adjusted disease load accumulates as the curve rises. The index was matched in time to each soil collection: pre-inoculation values (May) were zero, and the July and September disease values accumulated through 2 and 4 months after inoculation, respectively.
The resulting disease-weighted Ailanthus neighbourhood index (ANI
) at a soil point j was the sum, over all trees i in the same plot, of each tree’s baseline-adjusted AUDPC, scaled by its basal area and weighted by a common exponential distance-decay kernel:
is the Euclidean distance (m) between tree i and point j; DBH
is the stem diameter at breast height of tree i; α = 2 is the size exponent, so that each tree’s contribution is proportional to its basal area (∝ DBH²); and β = 0.15 m
is a common distance-decay constant shared by all trees, giving a mean weighting reach (1/β) of approximately 6.7 m. A heat-map of the resulting ANI
scores within each plot at each timepoint was generated for visualization (Figure 4).
To confirm that the findings did not hinge on these kernel settings, a sensitivity analysis was conducted wherein the index was recomputed across a grid of size exponents (α = 0, 1, 2)
), and all within-plot models were refit at each of 18 kernel combinations.

For each of the four bioassay species, three responses were analyzed: the proportion of seeds that emerged per tray, the mean total seedling length per tray (mm), and the mean root-to-shoot length ratio per tray. The analysis unit was the set of 45 point-by-month observations (15 soil points, each sampled in May, July, and September). Germination was available for all 45 observations, whereas length and ratio were defined only for trays containing at least one germinant and so were fit on fewer rows (n = 38–44, depending on species; Table 1). Each species was analyzed independently. For each species and response, the response was regressed on the standardized, time-matched disease influence index with plot held constant as a fixed nuisance term. Length and root-to-shoot ratio were fit with Gaussian linear models, and germination was fit as binomial seed counts, each tray of n sown seeds (n = 10, 100, 40, and 40 for black-eyed Susan, Johnsongrass, white snakeroot, and yarrow, respectively) forming a single binomial observation with a known denominator, so the three monthly trays at a point entered as separate rows without being collapsed and without reinflating the denominator. Because these seed counts were strongly overdispersed (Pearson ĉ ≈ 3–13), slope inference for germination used a quasibinomial model, which scales the standard errors by √ĉ while leaving the slope estimate unchanged.

was standardized to zero mean and unit variance, so its coefficient expresses the response change per standard deviation of disease influence. Plot was included as a fixed nuisance term that absorbs all among-plot differences. Only the slope on the standardized
index – the within-plot association between local disease influence and the response, with plot partialled out – was interpreted.

Because inoculation treatment is confounded with plot, no comparison among inoculation treatments or plots was made. For each species and response, the disease influence model was compared against a plot-only null using a small-sample information criterion: AICc for the Gaussian responses and its overdispersion-corrected analogue QAICc for the germination count ( ΔIC = IC(plot only) − IC(disease influence plus plot)) reported, so that positive values favor the disease influence model. Slopes are reported per standard deviation of the disease influence index, in response units for length and root-to-shoot ratio and on the log-odds scale for germination; standard errors, test statistics, two-sided p values, and the estimated Pearson overdispersion (ĉ) for the germination models are also reported (Table 1). A negative slope therefore indicates a smaller response where the modeled influence of
All analyses were conducted in R version 4.5.3 (R Core Team 2026).
Generative artificial-intelligence tools (Anthropic Claude, Opus 4.8) were used to assist with writing analysis code and with language editing during preparation of this manuscript.

plots, characterized from the 15 pooled samples collected at the time of inoculation (May 2024), were sandy and strongly to moderately acidic, with low cation exchange capacity and generally low nutrient availability. Water pH averaged 5.6 (range 4.9–6.1) and buffer pH 6.1 (5.9–6.3). Organic matter averaged 2.7% (1.4–4.6%), cation exchange

(2.7–6.9), and base saturation 62% (27–91%). Extractable phosphorus was uniformly low (mean 4 ppm; range 3–5 ppm), and potassium averaged 51 ppm (35–82 ppm). Extractable calcium and magnesium averaged 364 ppm (103–680 ppm) and 96 ppm (28–208 ppm), respectively. Micronutrients were generally low, averaging 1.1 ppm zinc (0.5–2.3 ppm), 17.6 ppm manganese (9.3–41.2 ppm), 0.2 ppm copper (0.1–0.3 ppm), 12.2 ppm iron (4.4–30.4 ppm), and 0.2 ppm boron (0.1–0.4 ppm); soluble salts were low throughout (67 ppm; 38–128 ppm). These properties are consistent with the sandy, acidic, and nutrient-poor soils characteristic of the regional Coastal Plain (Franzluebbers et al. 2025).

For each species and response, the slope of the bioassay response on the standardized disease influence index is reported, with plot held constant as a fixed nuisance term (Table 1). Of the twelve species-by-response associations, none reached significance at α = 0.05; two root-to-shoot-ratio slopes were marginal and were the only two favored over the plot-only null by ΔIC, while the remaining ten were statistically indistinguishable from zero.
The strongest associations were both in root-to-shoot ratio, though neither reached significance at α = 0.05. In
(yarrow), the ratio declined as modeled disease influence increased (−0.129 per SD of disease influence; t = −1.90, p = 0.065), one of the two slopes with positive ΔIC support
. A parallel, similarly marginal decline appeared in
(black-eyed Susan; −0.185 per SD; t = −1.95, P = 0.059, the lowest P among the twelve associations
(white snakeroot) showed no comparable trend (P = 0.46 and 0.76;

Neither of the other two responses tracked disease influence. Total seedling length was unrelated to the index in all four species (all P ≥ 0.29), and germination was likewise unrelated (all P ≥ 0.19). For every length and germination model the plot-only null was preferred over the disease influence model (ΔIC < 0; Table 1).

A reduced root-to-shoot ratio reflects proportionally less root elongation than shoot elongation, a shift more consistent with a modest change in the soil rooting environment than with a wholesale suppression of growth: total seedling length tracked the disease influence index in none of the four species, so the ratio change reflects altered allocation rather than smaller seedlings overall. This interpretation should nonetheless be read cautiously. With twelve regression models evaluated at α = 0.05, roughly one significant result is expected by chance alone, and the two lowest P values observed sit near that threshold. The repeated-measures structure of the bioassay, in which trays from the same plot and collection date contribute correlated observations, also tends to render the standard errors optimistic. The root-to-shoot signal is therefore treated as suggestive and provisional rather than established, and its variation among species, which the confounded plot design cannot support, is not interpreted.
), the qualitative outcome did not change: ten of the twelve species–response models were non-significant under every kernel (p ≥ 0.11), and the two root-to-shoot ratio trends identified at the

1) remained the only suggestive associations. The black-eyed Susan trend was stable across the grid (p = 0.05–0.07, with ΔIC favoring the disease term in every kernel), whereas the yarrow trend was kernel-dependent – strengthening to nominal significance under the broadest kernels (p = 0.03) and disappearing under the steepest (p = 0.33,
ΔIC < 0). This supports the finding that
inoculation has a nominal immediate effect on soil conditions,

the black-eyed Susan trend was insensitive to kernel choice whereas the yarrow trend was not, and given their marginal significance and the multiple-comparison context, neither is interpreted as an established biological effect.
That the bioassay responses were otherwise flat across the disease influence gradient is itself informative.

is known to alter the soils it occupies, shifting nutrient pools and the composition of soil microbial communities (Medina-Villar et al. 2016), and its tissues contain the allelochemical ailanthone, which suppresses germination and seedling growth in laboratory assays (Heisey 1996). Ailanthone is rapidly degraded in non-sterile soil, however, and its herbicidal activity under field conditions appears transient (Heisey and Heisey 2003, Gómez-Aparicio and Canham 2008). If the soil signature of

accumulates over years of litter input, root exudation, and microbial conditioning, a single season of disease-induced decline may be too brief to erode it. The near-absence of a germination or growth response to the disease influence index is consistent with a soil legacy that shifts slowly – over multiple seasons rather than weeks – even as the inoculated trees above it progress toward death.
Two considerations frame this single-season result. First, the pathways by which wilt could reshape these soils act gradually. As inoculated trees decline, the living inputs that distinguish

soils—root exudates and the allelochemical ailanthone delivered through root bark, litter, and fine-root turnover—diminish only slowly, while the countervailing pulse of decomposing root and shoot tissue accrues just as gradually as trees die and their tissues break down. Within a single growing season the inoculated trees were still standing and only beginning to express disease, so neither pathway would yet be expected to have substantially altered bulk soil. The flat bioassay response across the disease-influence gradient is therefore consistent with the timescale over which such a soil legacy would form or erode, rather than evidence that wilt leaves the soil unchanged.
Second, the absence of a soil-mediated signal bears on the safety case for

as an augmentative biological control. Host-range testing and risk assessments establish that the fungus poses limited direct pathogenic risk to non-target plants (Kasson et al. 2015, O’Neal and Davis 2015); the present results complement that work by addressing an indirect, soil-mediated pathway, indicating that in the first season after inoculation the treatment did not detectably change the soil’s capacity to support germination and early growth of co-occurring species. For an agent intended for operational release, a neutral near-term soil legacy is a desirable property.
For practitioners, this result is a reassuring one: over its first season, biological control of

did not measurably change the capacity of the underlying soil to support germination and early growth across four co-occurring herbaceous species (one grass and three forbs). Several limitations bound this conclusion. The bioassay was conducted ex situ on pooled, homogenized soil, which standardizes the assay but removes intact soil structure and living roots. The study also only spans one site and one growing season. Resolving whether biocontrol reshapes invaded soils will require following plots across multiple seasons after tree
mortality is complete, replicating inoculation treatments across independent plots, and pairing bioassays with direct measurements of soil chemistry and microbial community structure.

w:r.w:t: Figure 2. Soil sampling scheme in each plot. A 0.5 m circle was placed over plot center and 10 m from plot center in each cardinal direction. At these stations, five samples were collected from each station in the virulent and control plots; three samples were collected from each station in the attenuated plot. Samples were taken on May 6, July 1, and September 6, 2024.

w:r.w:t: . Bioassay seedling measurement images. A) Each homogenized, pooled soil sample was divided into four separate cells of a seed tray. B) After seven days, seedlings were teased from soil. C) The remaining soil was rinsed in a sifting screen to look for germinated seeds which had not emerged. D) Seedling roots and shoots were measured separately to the mm.
Within-plot relationships between the time-matched disease-influence index (ANI

; horizontal axis) and three bioassay responses—total seedling length, root-to-shoot ratio, and germination (as a proportion)—for the four bioassay species, with species in rows and responses in columns. Rows are (top to bottom)

(yarrow). Each point is one soil tray, with symbol shape denoting sampling month (May, July, or September) and color distinguishing the three plots, which enter the model only as a fixed nuisance block. Fitted lines are the within-plot relationships from a separate model of each response on the disease-influence index with plot held constant (plot partialled out): straight lines for total seedling length and root-to-shoot ratio (Gaussian linear models) and a logistic curve for germination (a binomial seed-count model with quasibinomial, overdispersion-corrected inference). Each panel is annotated with its within-plot slope per standard deviation of the index and the corresponding P value; the length and root-to-shoot-ratio slopes are in response units and the germination slope is on the log-odds scale. Because inoculation treatment is confounded with plot by design, the colors are not compared and no difference among plots or treatments is implied.
Within-plot regression results for each bioassay response on the standardized, time-matched disease influence index, fit separately for each species with plot held constant as a fixed nuisance term.
Competing interests: The author(s) declare none.
Brooks RK, Wickert KL, Baudoin A, Kasson MT, Salom SM. 2020. Field-inoculated
stands reveal the biological control potential of
in the mid-Atlantic region of the United States. Biol Control 148:104298.
Callaway RM, Ridenour WM. 2004. Novel weapons: invasive success and the evolution of increased competitive ability. Front Ecol Environ 2:436–443.
Ehrenfeld JG. 2003. Effects of exotic plant invasions on soil nutrient cycling processes. Ecosystems 6:503–523.
Fradin EF, Thomma BPHJ. 2006. Physiology and molecular aspects of Verticillium wilt diseases caused by V. dahliae and V. albo-atrum. Mol Plant Pathol 7:71–86.
[Reference entry needed: “Franzluebbers et al. 2025” is cited in Soil characteristics but is absent from Literature Cited — please add the full citation.]
Gómez-Aparicio L, Canham CD. 2008. Neighbourhood analyses of the allelopathic effects of the invasive tree
Heisey RM. 1996. Identification of an allelopathic compound from
(Simaroubaceae) and characterization of its herbicidal activity. Am J Bot 83:192–200.
Heisey RM, Heisey TK. 2003. Herbicidal effects under field conditions of
bark extract, which contains ailanthone. Plant Soil 256:85–99.
Inderbitzin P, Bostock RM, Davis RM, Usami T, Platt HW, Subbarao KV. 2011. Phylogenetics and taxonomy of the fungal vascular wilt pathogen
, with the descriptions of five new species. PLoS One 6:e28341.
Kasson MT, Short DPG, O’Neal ES, Subbarao KV, Davis DD. 2014. Comparative pathogenicity, biocontrol efficacy, and multilocus sequence typing of
Kasson MT, O’Neal ES, Davis DD. 2015. Expanded host range testing for
: potential biocontrol agent against the invasive
Kasson MT, Kasson LR, Wickert KL, Davis DD, Stajich JE. 2019. Genome sequence of a lethal vascular wilt fungus,
, a biological control used against the invasive
